dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, responding for overdose/poisoning at a home in the city of [CITY].
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call for help today?
My friend is unconscious, and he snorted heroin before he passed out.
Patient unresponsive with no response to verbal or physical stimuli.
Pinpoint pupils in both eyes and non-reactive to light.
No discernible motor response despite verbal or physical stimuli attempted.
Agonal respirations observed-very minimal and ineffective breathing with initial SpO2 at 60-65% in room air.
Initial respiratory assessment noted agonal respirations before improvement following BVM administration. Current rate on monitor is 12 breaths per minute.
Fast and irregular pulse detected-initial palpation at radial seems 110 bpm. Current monitor read is 131 bpm.
EKG shows rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation, confirmed with lead III deprived of cf.
Blood pressure is 182/115 mmHg-elevated and concerning.
SpO2 reads 96% following BVM administered with O2 at 15 LPM.
Administering 4-lead continuous cardiac monitoring for assessment support.
Inserting IV line-18G in left AC with saline lock for rapid medication access.
Administering 0.5 mg Naloxone intravenously after IV access established.
Blood pressure has decreased slightly to 176/124 now.
The patient snorted a small amount of heroin and had been drinking all night before passing out.
He has a past history of alcohol dependence but is not a regular illicit drug user.
Respiratory rate is now 15 per minute and effort remains shallow.
Performing 12-lead EKG-atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response noted on first tracing.
Heart rate increased to 135 bpm with BP of 176/124.
No ST elevation on EKG but atrial fibrillation still visible.
Respiratory rate is 19 per minute, slightly increased and now more stable.
SpO2 reading is 95%-slight improvement from earlier readings.
Heart rate has dropped to 122 bpm and BP at 154/107. Rhythm still irregular.
Patient is now alert and oriented-responding to questions.
Blood pressure has dropped to 154/107 mmHg-continuing improvement over time.
Respiratory rate is 18 per minute now-breathing clearly improved.
Rhythm now shows sinus tachycardia with stable HR monitoring.
Heart rate has dropped to 114 bpm, but sinus still tachycardic.
Monitor now shows 104 bpm, with rhythm not yet confirmed as normal sinus.
Patient has returned to GCS of 15 and is A&Ox4.
Any past medical history like high BP-has a self-reported history of elevated BP but no diagnosis of HTN.
Airway-patient is maintaining it now with effective breathing and no obstruction signs.
Capnography readings went from 70-80 initially to within normal limits after assisted breathing.
Any known allergies, including Tylenol or others?
He's allergic to Tylenol and no other known medications.
Transporting the patient directly to [COMPANY_NAME] Emergency Department.
Understood. Give him the best care.
Log rolling patient onto the Reeves and securing straps for transfer.
Mr. Christopher is breathing without assistance and showing overall improvement.
Latest vitals recorded: BP 154/107, HR 114, SpO2 97, RR 19.
Currently in sinus tach, still under continuous monitoring during transport.
No reported pain, neurological deficit or injuries to be noted at this time.
Patient reports lifelong elevated BP without formal diagnosis or treatment.
ABCs remain self-maintained without need for intervention during relocation.
Patient is now fully oriented and responding to voice and touch.
Patient is maintaining airway, breathing and circulation independently.
No observed compromise of ABCs at this time.
Vital monitoring showing good stability with no signs of acute distress.
BP readings and rhythm are improving per 12-lead EKG.
Patient denies any other significant medical conditions on review.
IV line remains patent at the 18G access in the left AC.
No signs of deterioration or unexpected changes in condition.
Patient is fully secured on the stretcher using all safety restraints.
Indication for transport remains stable and allows for semi-Fowler's transfer.
Per protocol medical overdose handling-patient is stable for transport.
No further interventions required in the field-handing over care to the ED.
Final vitals noted at time of transfer: 132/92, 104 HR, 95 SpO2, 17 RR.
Full care administered as per protocol 7-1 for overdose/poisoning.
IV and EKG monitoring are fully documented and available for the ED to review.
All indicated transport and ED protocols have now been executed.
Transfer is now complete with patient in stable condition throughout.
Capnography is normal and remains within expected limits for transport.
Oxygen, BVM, and EKG monitor are intact and working.
Patient stays A&Ox4 and responds to verbal interaction at all times during transport.
No new symptoms or complications during the transfer period.
Final vitals unchanged from last monitored at time of ED arrival.
Small amount of heroin is noted as the reported substance ingested.
All field documentation has been completed and handed over.
Naloxone administered, IV placed, EKG monitoring, and all actions recorded per protocol.
All EMS care provided under field standing orders as required.
All key assessment findings documented for the Emergency Department team.
Transport classification: cold during transfer to the ED.
No signs of discomfort or acute pain reported or observed at any time.
Emergency contact notes provided to the hospital for follow-up coordination.
ED team confirmed that the patient is receiving appropriate ongoing follow-up.
Blood sugar levels were not recorded during the emergency encounter.
All ABC assessments during initial and repeated assessments indicated self-sufficiency.
Final vitals and condition align with documented patient status prior to hospital handover.
